title: Screen Printing — Squeegee Pressure and Ink Rheology
content: Screen printing forces ink through a mesh screen (80–230 threads/cm) via a squeegee. Ink transfer depends on: squeegee hardness (55–70 Shore A), angle (60–75°), speed (100–300 mm/s), and ink viscosity (1000–5000 cP thixotropic).
extended_content: Automated screen printing: 1) Register — substrate clamped on platen, screen lowered (snap-off gap 2–3 mm). 2) Flood — squeegee fills mesh open areas. 3) Print — squeegee stroke deposits ink. 4) Screen lift — substrate separates. Mesh count selection: coarse mesh (80 mesh) for thick inks/plastisol; fine mesh (200 mesh) for fine detail/water-based inks. Squeegee pressure: 2–5 N/cm (too high → premature mesh wear and ink bleeding; too low → pinholes). Motor drive: DC gear motor with encoder for speed control; pneumatic cylinder for squeegee pressure; stepper for registration. Mesh tensioning: 20–25 N/cm Newton via tensioning roller. Registration accuracy: ±0.25 mm with stepper-driven platen.
units: pressure: N/cm, viscosity: cP, speed: mm/s
source: Leach & Pierce, The Printing Ink Manual, 5th ed.; SGIA/FESPA Screen Printing Handbook; Kipphan, Handbook of Print Media
